The tremor has split my largest block of Carrara marble right down the center.
I must salvage the clay models before the floodwaters ruin the delicate armatures.
These fallen ruins provide a strange, jagged geometry that I could carve into something.
My chisels are buried under a mountain of plaster and shattered stone from the ceiling.
I can use the river's silt to rebuild the forms that the wind swept away.
The light from that collapsing star casts shadows that reveal the deepest flaws in my marble block.
I must find a way to carve the terrifying curvature of that void into a permanent form.

The sudden gravity shift is pulling the clay right off my wheel and toward the horizon.
I will spend my remaining hours attempting to capture the exact geometry of that celestial rupture.
This limestone vein extends right into your property, and I cannot leave the block unfinished.
I need a few more inches of this yard to set up my heavy armature properly.
Your fence is blocking the natural light I need to see the depth of these carvings.
If we move the marker, I can preserve the integrity of this marble outcrop for my work.
I cannot move my studio equipment without risking a crack in the sculpture's delicate base.
The way the light bends around the rim suggests a density that would resist any chisel.
I wonder if the surface of that void possesses a grain I could carve into.
This iridescent glow casts shadows that would be impossible to replicate in marble or clay.
I must touch the edges to see if the portal feels like polished stone or something fluid.
If I could capture the negative space of that aperture, it would be my greatest masterpiece.
These thick vines are like unworked clay, blocking my path with an unruly, organic mass.
I must use my chisel to carve a narrow passage through these stubborn, interlocking branches.
The way the light filters through the canopy creates a dramatic chiaroscuro across the forest floor.
I see a limestone outcrop ahead that possesses the perfect grain for a monumental relief.
Every jagged rock and twisting root in this brush feels like a study in raw form.
The only sound remaining is the rhythmic chip of my chisel against the marble.
I have forgotten the noise of the city while carving these deep, hollow veins.
The dust settles in heavy layers across the studio during these endless, quiet months.
My hands know the curve of the stone better than I know any voice.
This solitude allows me to hear the hidden fractures deep within the limestone block.
Please step away from the marble, or I will be forced to defend my work.
I will use this heavy mallet to crush your hand if you touch my clay.
Do not dare throw your stones at the delicate carvings I spent months perfecting.
Get back before I drive this steel chisel right through your threatening posture.
My armature is fragile, so stay back or I will fight you with my tools.
This block of Carrara marble is the finest grain I have ever encountered in my studio.
I spent three months chiseling the delicate folds of the drapery to capture that perfect movement.
The way the light hits the polished surface of this bronze cast is exactly as I envisioned.
My grandfather's old steel rasps are the only tools I trust for the final smoothing of the clay.
I cannot bear to let this piece leave my sight until the sealant has completely cured on the stone.
This block of Carrara marble is far too small to carve the full length of the figure.
I must be incredibly precise with my chisel because there is no way to put the stone back.
If I crack this vein now, I will have wasted the only piece of alabaster I own.
My supply of fine-grit sandpaper is nearly gone and I still have the entire torso to smooth.
I cannot afford to make a single mistake with this remaining sliver of rare obsidian.
